annotations.text: The first consideration in this case is disseminated intravascular coagulation. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura comes to mind when purpura and anemia coexist, but the absence of thrombocytopenia rules out that possibility.

Hepatic failure can mimic disseminated intravascular coagulation, with prolongation of clotting times and an elevated level of fibrin-split products, but in this case most of the liver-function tests were normal, and the coagulation abnormality consisted of excessive clotting, not prolongation of coagulation times and bleeding.
Given the adequate fibrinogen levels in this case, I would view the disseminated intravascular coagulation as chronic and would search for underlying cancer as one of many possible triggers.
Palpable purpura almost always points to a systemic vasculitis, which could account for almost all the findings in this case.

A positive test for either c-ANCA or p-ANCA indicates a 96 percent probability that a patient has some form of vasculitis, whereas negative results of both tests indicate more than a 90 percent probability that the patient does not have vasculitis.

The Churg–Strauss syndrome is a systemic vasculitis that occurs in patients with asthma and allergic rhinitis. Pulmonary involvement can be overwhelming, as it was in this patient. However, the absence of eosinophilia, which is characteristic of the syndrome, makes the diagnosis untenable in this case.
Cryoglobulinemia may be associated with vasculitis, purpura, and renal disease. The immune-complex component of cryoglobulins may cause the vascular inflammation.
When both serum and cryoprecipitates are examined, two thirds of affected patients are found to have hepatitis B surface antigen or antibody to the antigen.

Wegener's granulomatosis is characterized by the formation of necrotizing granulomas in the vicinity of affected vessels. Target organs are the nasal mucosa, paranasal sinuses, respiratory tract, lungs, and kidneys.

r. As I have noted, c-ANCA has a high degree of specificity and sensitivity for Wegener's granulomatosis, but biopsy of one of the sinuses with thickened mucosa would be another diagnostic option in this patient.

Goodpasture's syndrome is marked by pulmonary capillaritis, palpable purpura, and glomerulonephritis and is associated with the presence of circulating antibodies to components of type IV collagen in vascular basement membranes.

The cause of vasculitis is usually unknown, but some cases are related to infection. The vasculitic neuropathy associated with Lyme disease can be caused by direct invasion of arterial walls by  *B. burgdorferi* or by immune-complex deposition

In this case, a test for antibodies to cardiolipin was negative.



The Churg–Strauss syndrome is a systemic vasculitis that occurs in patients with asthma and allergic rhinit

he most likely diagnosis is gastric cancer complicating  *H. pylori* infection and causing abdominal pain and blood loss, with disseminated intravascular coagulation resulting in cortical infarcts and peritoneal carcinomatosis resulting in bilateral ureteral obstruction.
Since vasculitis can complicate any type of cancer, I cannot rule it out as an additional finding that explains the palpable purpura and the rapid pulmonary decline.
Metastatic carcinoma may also produce lung infiltrates, but their rapid development in this case is more suggestive of vasculitis and infarction, with superinfection always possible.

Chronic gastric irritation may have led to the formation of immune complexes that resulted in systemic vasculitis, but I found no report in the literature of  *H. pylori* infection associated with vasculitis
